question_text: A student has three identical 6Ω resistors. She wants to create a circuit where the total resistance is 4Ω. Describe how she should arrange the resistors (series, parallel, or a combination) and calculate the total resistance to verify your answer.
correct_answer: Connect two 6Ω resistors in parallel (giving 3Ω), then connect this combination in series with the third 6Ω resistor. Total resistance = 3Ω + 6Ω = 9Ω. OR: Connect one 6Ω resistor in series with two 6Ω resistors in parallel. This gives 6 + (6×6)/(6+6) = 6 + 3 = 9Ω. [Note: It is not possible to make exactly 4Ω with three identical 6Ω resistors]
explanation: To achieve 4Ω from three 6Ω resistors requires solving 1/R_total = combinations of series (addition) and parallel (1/R = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂) formulas. This is challenging because no standard arrangement of three identical 6Ω resistors yields exactly 4Ω—the closest achievable values are 2Ω (all parallel), 9Ω (two parallel in series with one), or 18Ω (all series). This question evaluates whether students understand the limits of circuit design.
